The film begins with a single shot that awakens a nostalgia that’s hard to explain, almost inexplicable. From the very first seconds, it reveals itself as a film meant to be watched from beginning to end without interruptions — the kind that asks you to disconnect from everything, put your phone away, and focus entirely on the story.

The performances by George Clooney and Adam Sandler are excellent. I couldn’t expect anything less from both actors, the direction, and the entire ensemble that brings this film together. Each character is well developed and contributes to a narrative about choices, maturity, friendship, and the impact certain moments have on how we see life.

In my interpretation, the film gives the impression that it dialogues with the idea that “life is a constant oscillation between the desire to have and the boredom of possessing,” as described by Arthur Schopenhauer. That’s the feeling it left me with: we spend our lives wanting to achieve something, and when we finally do, we often realize it doesn’t fulfill us the way we imagined.

This reflection connects powerfully with Jay Kelly’s final line: “let’s watch it again, I want to watch it again.” The strongest part of this ending is that it’s neither overly sad nor overly optimistic. It’s human. It leaves you sitting in silence, staring at the screen after it ends, thinking. And to me, that’s the sign of a very well-resolved ending 🎬

A well-made movie - and entertaining - so it get plus marks for that.  However, it does depict Hollywood stars in a way that is not flattering at all.  That is to say, Clooney is portrayed here with lots of pretend problems.  But not included among those problems is having access to lots of money, along with private jets at his beck and call.   It's hard to relate to someone who's biggest issue is whether to take a lavish trip to Italy to pick up a "Tribute Award" - or to spend more time with his family.  Most people can't relate to this sort of a crisis - and maybe that's the point of this movie.  Which is: Don't be a Hollywood actor - because you'll end up shallow, and alone - and wonder why you've spent only 5 minutes with your real family during a 35 year career.  At least he sort of realizes this outcome by the end of the film.  So redemption?  Maybe.  Also, this movie has the most restrained performance from Adam Sandler I've ever seen - which is probably an acting stretch for him - and a welcome change from the usual characters he plays.
